turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments next in Rosewell v. LaSalle National Bank. Mr. Hauser, you may proceed whenever you're ready.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: My name is Henry Hauser and I'm an Assistant State's Attorney, and I represent the petitioners in this certiorari Certiorari was granted to the 7th Circuit last March. This is a 1983 tax injunction case that was filed in the federal court and it deals with 1977 local real estate taxes which are assessed and contended to be unconstitutionally assessed on the taxpayer in the case at bar. The response of the county tax officials and that's other things on the federal anti-tax injunction statute. The motion to dismiss which is set out at Appendix, page 11, also sets out grounds that there was not a claim stated, and that there was available to the taxpayer, again, a plain, speedy, and efficient remedy in the state courts, and also a remedy available pursuant to the state statutory tax system. Secondly, it was contended in that motion that the state taxpayer had available to her in this case a 1983 action in which she could have raised all of the claims which she de­ sired to raise in the federal court injunctive action. referring to, now? a specific objection in the Collector's annual application for the sale of delinquent tax property. So that she clearly had available to her the statutory Illinois proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, her response to that in part is that since the State pays no interest on the tax while the refund's being litigated, that that's not an efficient remedy. What do you have to say about that?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, our response is that the taxpayer would have a right to file a 1983 action in a state court raising that precise claim. So that even if that claim was that the Illinois Supreme Court has not had a chance -- in fact, it's not been given a chance -- to rule on the 1983 portion of a claim to interest. is basically the Illinois Supreme Court's holding in

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You would call five years' delay a speedy remedy?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, it's speedy in the sense that if the taxpayer — it's available to the taxpayer, and the taxpayer has a damage remedy that at least a taxpayer who can pay has a damage remedy available to her. And if the -- I think the ruling of the district court was based on Tully v. Griffin. The question in the anti-tax injunction statute is whether there would be a federal right which would otherwise So the question comes down to, and it's a question that's not been resolved in Illinois, and the question is-- and it's, I don't think, presented in this case -- whether there is a injunction against the delay or the harm that's caused to the taxpayer. Now if the taxpayer has available to her in this case, as she did, a federal damage remedy, or --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is the federal damage remedy? What will that give her? Doesn't that raise the same question? She only gets interest if she has a constitutional right to the interest.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, she would have to show that, judge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't think that responds to my ques­ tion that one might assume there's no federal constitutional right to interest, and nevertheless feel that a remedy which denies interest on withheld money for a five-year period is not an adequate remedy, within the meaning of the statute.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, again we argue the policy that under Tully v. Griffin, as long as long as the taxpayer if there's a constitutional right to interest.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, then, what's the -- I question the . policy behind the other side of the argument that there is some reason why there should be, why that must lay?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the reason would be that an adequate remedy ought to make the litigant who's been harmed whole. That would be the reasoning, I suppose.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, the damage remedy certainly does that, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, five years at 15 percent a year, or even ten, to make it easier to compute, at least for me, that doesn't make them very whole, does it?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, let me refocus that. If the taxpayer can demonstrate an unconstitutional overassessment and if in fact the interest is a measure of damages, then the taxpayer will have a right to interest, and a taxpayer is In the argument that lawsuit has indeed already been filed and that cause of action basically acknowledged by the 7th Circuit. So we have a disappearance here of -- you have a federal tax injunction where the taxpayer has available to her a federal damage remedy. I need to make one thing clear --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But only predicated on a constitutional violation?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, that's so; that's so. But why else? In other words, the policy is, the real question is the policy behind the federal injunction for state taxes.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: And we respectfully submit that the risk -- the complaint alleges, and I need to clarify this, the complaint alleges a two-year delay, not a five-year delay.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: The calendar of the federal court? It's probably as crowded as any federal district court in the nation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is it slower than -- is it as slow as the state court?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, we don't have any federal tax cases, Your Honor, with which we can measure this. This is a leading case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the civil calendar generally?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: That I couldn't say, Your Hcnor. That

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, now, what's the interval from the filing of the complaint to trial in the federal court?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: I have no idea, Your Honor. The record doesn't show it. I wish I could help you out there but I can't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's probably pretty long, isn't it? to interest. Isn't that correct?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Except that her position is that she --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know what her position is, but your position is that she's not entitled to interest. Isn't that correct ?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Under Illinois law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under Illinois law or under the Federal Constitution?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, we argue that also.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. You say she's not entitled to her lawsuit as the reason why she should lose her lawsuit?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, if she can demonstrate the right to the interest, she ought not -- unless she can demonstrate the right to interest --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, I know, but you don't concede she has any right to interest.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: I couldn't, Your Honor, because we op­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then she wouldn't -- you can turn that argument on its head too, and say that if she can't demonstrate that she has a right to what she's claiming, she's not entitled to the injunction that she's claiming, not be­ cause of the anti-injunction statute, but because of a short­ coming in her pleadings.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: We argue that too, Your Honor. We argue that the allegations that are made in the complaint are not sufficient to demonstrate a violation of her Fourteenth Amendment rights. Now -- she get a hearing? And you're arguing -- you certainly are arguing the merits of her case but isn't the question whether she should be able to get an earlier hearing in a federal court than she could in the state? statutes and refund statutes, there is no interest allowable in those, is there?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Not -- the way --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that right or not?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: As a matter of Illinois statutory construction, that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right. So you rely on the availabil­ ity of an adequate remedy in the state courts on 1983?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Or the availability in federal court of a similar damage remedy which is the predicate for our remedies, because the federal court had no right to make federal remedies for the state courts under diversity juris­ diction . But under 1341, and under the Civil Rights Act, where you have a jurisdictional predicate based on federal statute, then the question has to go back really to the historical precedent. Cases like Matthews v. Rodgers., and Great Lakes Dredge S Dock v. Huffman. And Tully v. Griffir. is a situation where that question is met. Ahd the articulation of the equity rule is that a federal court is under an equi­ table duty to refrain from interfering with a state's collec­ tion of its revenue except in cases where an assertive federal right might otherwise be lost. Now, the question is where --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Hauser, it's a little -late in the argument, but how are you going to get under 1983?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, that's my question too, Your

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm looking at page 2a of the certiorari petition.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, all of a sudden, on the argument, we have three questions. How'd that happen?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, here's how it happened, Your Honor. The --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One of those questions is the one to which my brother Marshall has just directed your attention.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Yes. One of those has split, and the economy. Now, I pointed out, and I did argue and I did set out in my brief that we were raising a question that arguably might not be available below, and we did argue in our brief that we perceived the question to be subsumed within that question. So —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then the only question presented in the certiorari petition and the only question that the Court of Appeals undertook to decide was whether or not there was a plain, speedy, and efficient remedy in the state courts. Is that correct? HR. HAUSER: My response to that is that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that correct?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, that's the question that they decided, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. And the only question presented on your petition for certiorari.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. Whether or not there was a plain, speedy, and efficient remedy in the state courts.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But your point is, your question did in­ clude two alternative state remedies, is it not?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Yes, it really did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but it didn't include the propriety of a federal 1983 action.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor, although that's a fundamental question which is -- different question from the one embraced in your certiorari petition on which we granted.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: It is a different question, although I think that if there is federal jurisdiction lacking, again, the district court ruled in the favor of the County, on grant­ cause of action.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, the difficulty --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which is rather normal, isn't it?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, the difficulty is that the usual way that these tax cases go is under a 1341 analysis of the federal tax injunction statute. And the other questions get subsumed, in fact, lost. And if there are underlying diffi­ culties and those cases present important issues, then it's the underlying issues that that case is going to be cited as authority for. tant not to raise for the court, to give Your Honors a chance to rule on it. And we indicated that we were raising the issues that had not been particularly presented to the district court. Fundamentally, the problem that I need to return to is the relationship between 1341 and the historical overview taxpayer will not suffer irreparable injury, that is, that the taxpayer will not suffer an injury which can't be recompensed by money -- and we're talking about money here; we're not talking about the loss of the taxpayer's property. In other words, because the taxpayer had available to her the state the damage remedy, because she was able to pay, a state refund remedy, there is no threat in this case of loss of the property. If the property isn't lost, then we're only talking about interest, and if that's the federal right that's going to be lost, then the taxpayer can certainly assert that right, then we respectfully suggest that under Tully v. Griffin there is no right to a federal injunction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is a 1983 action available in state courts in this kind of tax case?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, we don't know that, Your Honor, because it's never been filed. The — don't know whether it will or won't, does that bear on the plaintiff's speedy and efficient remedy?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Yes. Well, Hillsborough v. Cromwell says that if the remedy in the state court is uncertain, then 1341 is avoided. But our argument is that the facts in Hillsborough were a situation where the uncertainty was not the result of a failure of any taxpayers to file the suit. In other words, you can't establish a state remedy, particularly a new remedy under 1983 unless some taxpayers file it. And there's never been a 1983 tax case filed in the state court, federal court. It's only after cases when people start thinking along the lines of filing 1983 cases in the state court, as occurred in Martinez, and it's occurred in Thiboutat, that you have this clash —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: My question really is, it's clear that the action is available in the federal court; no doubt about with allegations very similar to this case. We respectfully submit that the analysis has to be in view of the disrup­ tion that's created to a state tax system by a tax injunction, where tax machinery is disrupted, where there's an obstruction of revenue, perhaps damaging the budget, where there's a risk, of taxpayer insolvency which is shifted to the state, and where there are state law questions that need to get sorted out all along. Those are all factors which were set out in your Perez concurrence in the dissenting opinion. damages threatened to the state , as against what we might con­ sider the best Illinois damage remedy. And if the fact is that the taxpayer will not suffer any irreparable injury, that the taxpayer will not lose her property and must merely wait to be recompensed by damages for a claim which she must estab­ lish under Tully, because if she can't establish the claim un­ If there was no

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, would you be resisting the 1983 suit, seeking only damages in federal court3

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: We resisted that in federal court and we lost in the 7th Circuit and certiorari was denied. I think with twojustices dissenting from the denial. So the law as it stands -- and we accept the law as it stands -- is that there is a federal damage remedy that's aVd-ilable in the nor­ thern district of Illinois for any money damages that we felt

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it's an availability under federal law. And do you have any authority that 1341 should be con­ strued to include remedies in state courts under federal law as well as under state law?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: After all, it will be federal law, won't it? proceed under federal law?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Right. That's right. And I think that supports my position, because when the Illinois courts look to the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, I don't know. It's a question of con­ struction of 1341.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, yes --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A remedy in the state courts. Does it mean under federal law as well as under state law?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, here!s what I would say to that -- answer is, yes.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: The answer is that the state court will look to federal law, much the same way that federal courts look

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if the remedy you're relying on to get you out from under 1341 is a damage suit in federal court, it simply doesn't come within the language of 1341.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. And the reason why that's so is because initially 1341 was directed to diversity actions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, however it may be, you can't invoke 1341 by saying that she has an adequate damage remedy in federal court. Because it doesn't come in under the terms of 1341. Griffin,, a 1341 case that under the broader view of equity law, that a federal court is under an equitable duty to refrain from interfering with a state's collection of its revenue, except in cases where a federal right may otherwise be lost, and the dispensing injunctions, you will end up with a plain, speedy, and efficient federal damage remedy, as we have in this case, that will --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there might be reasons not to issue an injunction in various cases, depending upon --

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In any particular case, depending upon the generally applicable maximus of equity with respect to irre­ parable damage and no adequate remedy at law and the rest of it But the question in this case is whether or not such an in­ it?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, no. We think the question is broader because of the language in Tully v. Griffin, in Tully v. Griffin. And that's because you have the limitation of 1341. And it ,at least starts out being a limitation of diver­ sity jurisdiction. As you start creating more federal rights,

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: I have a case, I cited it in my brief, Your Honor, Alberty v. Daniel. And Alberty v. Daniel indicates the willingness of Illinois courts to accept 1983 --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I didn't say, willingness; I said, that says it can be done.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, if Your Honor demands a case, me to demonstrate a case that's never been filed, I can't. And no taxpayer --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, isn't that what the statute re­ quires ? that is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I think the statute requires either a case or a statute which says that the courts of Illinois are open for this point, 1983. And you don't have either.

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: The only point I would make, Your Honor, This argument that you're now making really depends on our agreeing with the 7th Circuit decision in the Fulton Market case, doesn't it?

turns.name: HAUSER
turns.text: Yes, that's correct, Your Honor. If there's a disagreement with that, then that changes the avail­ ability of remedies altogether. Thank you very much, Your Honors.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Fox?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: As it has been remarked, there is one basic question before the Court today. That is, is there a plain, speedy, and efficient remedy at hand, available without doubt Cromwell -- in the courts of Illinois? Counsel has maintained the nonpayment of interest does not render a remedy inadequate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you equate inadequate with inefficient?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, sir. "Inadequate" has been used commonly in the federal court, sir, Mr. Chief Justice, with the "PS&E," plain, speedy, and efficient. I believe if we go back into cases like Great. Lakes and into Matthews v. Rodgers and the extensions of those, "plain, speedy, and efficient" has been declared coterminous with or the same, has the same meaning as "adequate." against the United States in which no interest is allowed. Not in this context, but would you say that that's not an adequate remedy that might lay a foundation for some other type of relief such as equitable relief?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Sir, in the context of the United States, first of all, we do have the question of the sovereign. And interest allowable against the sovereign is by statute, as witness the Internal Revenue Code.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, haven't you got a sovereign here?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: We are not claiming, Mr. Chief Justice, that Illinois is compelled to award interest. That question have to award interest on tax refunds. But if it does not, it then opens its door to federal jurisdiction by not provid­ ing a plain, speedy, and efficient remedy. iIn the 7th Circuit we had the same thing in Olin Mathieson.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there's a certain circularity to that, though, counsel, isn't there? Because if it opens the doors to federal courts but is not required to pay interest, all you get is the same relief in federal court as you would have gotten in state court, that is, your refund without

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: No, sir, because we do not sue for a re­ fund -- 1341 is directed only to injunctive relief and this case stands on .that particular proposition which we have allegec. We seek injunctive relief and we maintain that injunctive re­ lief is the only relief which will maintain this taxpayer whole. We maintain that if we're going to keep it -- a lien for taxes affixes against a person's property on the first day of January in any particular tax year, and therefore amounts to a taking, as it were, of the taxpayer's property without due process of law, and it of course subsumes the equal protection --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're -- the lack of provision for interest is simply the reason why the remedy is inadequate?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: The remedy is inadequate. As I said before --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the merits of your constitutional

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: I think they are procedurally admitted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but the only question is, whether or not 1341 bars an injunction.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Exactly. constitutional right to interest. We are not litigating that particular question today.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But then, why should it be of any import­ ance in the administration of the anti-injunction statute that a state doesn't allow interest, if you have no constitutional right to it?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Because, I believe that to take a man's money today — to paraphrase Judge Learned Hand about 50 years ago in the Procter S Gamble case, to take a man's money today as a condition of his going into court and being able to sue same money, is giving back less than the state took.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, even though it is not a constitu­ tional violation, it brings into play, it relieves you from the bar of 1341?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, sir. Now, it would be a second story if we were to go into the condition which obtains in the finan­ cial world today -- and it is not without some degree >of irony that the day on which this Court granted certiorari the prime average taxpayer, of course, at prime. Now, is it -- and this is a second question, or a meanwhile allowing the state to use that taxpayer's money to generate interest for the state which the county treasurer is allowed to do? QUESTION Now you're talking about a Fifth Amend- ment taking?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, sir. It is not in this case and I don't mean to go far afield.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but you've said it's not in this case, but you've put it in the case.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: I apologize —

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: -- because one of the justices had asked me -- QUESTION I'm not saying this critically.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: I see. QUESTION I hope it's analytically.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: It is .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I thought you said a minute ago that Illinois was not required to pay interest?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: It is not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then did I misunderstand you just now in saying that it is?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I .mean, .what's less, less than what? You said they got back less. Why less?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Why? Because let's merely assume a 12 percent inflation rate and give it back in two years, you're: giving back 75 cents for the dollar you took. cause you were denied interest.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Because of inflation. That's one aspect, one prong. The other prong is this, as Judge Learned Hand

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose one relevant question is, what the Congress had in mind in what an inadequate remedy might be. Wasn't it, historically, the notion that interest isn't allowable against the government, absent some specific statute:

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: That is correct. There is a -- if you go down and make a tally of the states, some do,, some don't. Man^ of the states which don't allow interest allow declaratory or injunctive relief. In fact, even in Illinois, sir, in the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is there any -- have you got any evidence at all, any legislative history or any other indica­ was tantamount to the lack of a speedy and adequate remedy?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: No, sir, the legislative history of 1341 which I have been able to read is not indicative of that par­ ticular point. It merely recites the problem of out-of-state I think the 7th Circuit, Mr. Chief Justice, used the term "plain" in its decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'm not sure what it means. Plain or fancy wouldn't make much difference. The important thing is whether it's speedy and whether it's adequate. And speedy and adequate are really interrelated, aren't they?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: I believe so; yes. I think they are sub­ sumed, that speedy is subsumed under the word adequate, which seems to be more generic.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If it were speedy so that you got it in dollar and the inflation and the use of the money.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: On the other hand -- I would not say that either, sir, quite; because if we're looking at a basic con­ stitutional deprivation to begin with, somehow or other that For instance: let us suppose that this constitutional depriva­ tion of high taxes or high assessments were motivated by ethnic: or religious or racial considerations on the part of the Assessor of Cook County, is it proper relief, is it adequate relief to say, oh, go ahead, Mr. Polish, or Mr. Irish, or Mr. Italian taxpayer, in this particular neighborhood, pay your taxes in full under protest, sue for refund, and then in 60 specify, if it's a tax -- if it's limited to taxation.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: I think, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, that the fact that this insult occurs as a result of a constitutional deprivation removes so-called irreparable injury, removes this period of time, pure time. For instance -- and I would refer, let's say, to the case of Henry v. Greenville Airport, which was cited in our brief, which states that when a constitutional right is invaded like this, that questions of irreparable injury and the like are not even properly considered.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But 1341 purports to impose a prohibition over and above the normal injunctive precautions, doesn't it?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: That is right. And again, however, we are in a theoretical area when we say, if 'the next day you could get your money back -- which is absolutely impossible under the Cook County collection procedures, because you pay your taxes over a period of six months and are not able to sue until, about nine months after the taxes have been paid in the first instance. adequate remedy, immediate injunction at the state level to provide an adequate remedy, or some other remedy than requiring a taxpayer, as here, to pay 3-1/2 times the just taxes merely to have the right to go in to sue to have them come back.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you say the state remedy contemplated by 1341 must be injunctive in nature? That a payment under protest and ultimate refund, even in a very short period of time, isn't adequate?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: That's right. My own personal opinion, tax refunds, commensurate with the market today, I do not thinl that we would be before this Court today.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you didn't do it because you knew you Fulton Market Cold Storage Company at the appellate level and then at the Supreme Court level, again a case cited in our brief. And as recently as this last year in Chrysler Corpora­ tion v. Gunderson, the Illinois court, the appellate court, aware of the decision of the 7th Circuit in this case, said, furthermore, our own state Supreme Court's clear rulings in Lakefront and Lorenz, and Clarendon, stating that the remedy is adequate without interest, remain controlling precedent on us, as on all Illinois courts, unless reversed by that court or

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That case is not in your brief, is it, counsel?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Chrysler Corporation?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: It's in the response to the petition, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. foreign corporations were coming into the several states and enjoining state tax collections. That's in Senator Bone's remarks in the legislative history. I think there are -- at least through that entire discussion. It is not a protection, really, as/or against citizens of the state itself bringing actions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but the Act doesn't make any dis­ tinction, does it?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: No. It does not. But the genesis which you referred to is found in the problems of big interstate it locally, and if you recall at that time, they couldn't use 1343 because this was all pre-Household v. Lynch.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you, if you paid the tax and on your constitutional argument, your colleague suggested that the state would pay interest.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: No, sir, because the State has unequivo­ cally stated in all --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but I thought the State had said only that without adjudicating the constitutional point. Suppose the state courts had themselves decided that the assessment was unconstitutional, and that you deserved a refund. Have the state courts then said there would be no interest payable? the Illinois law is, no interest without a statute providing for interest, and there is no statute in Illinois.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whether or not the assessment is consti­ tutional ?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: That’s right. Furthermore, the Illinois Legislature has had before it twice within the last four or Legislature has turned down such a statute twice within the last three or four legislative sessions. I will not address, because of Mr. Justice Stewart's interrogation of my colleague here, the collateral issues which were raised on this particular, in the briefs, such as as the 1343 argument and the other arguments that were raised. Counsel has adverted, however, on several occasions to the question of the state remedies of 1983. There is no grounds for a state 1983 remedy. In fact in a damage action, as Mr. Justice Marshall pointed out, there just isn't any case to which you can point. There is no rule, of course, as Note 1 in Maine v. Thiboutat pointed out, that the state must hear a 1983 action, and I can think of nothing more intrusive by the federal judiciary into the state than to require the State of Illinois, let's say, first to hear that kind of 1983 action; secondly, to grant an injunction or damages; thirdly, perhaps, to grant attorneys' fees, which are not provided for under the statute, or to pay interest, or to waive its exhaust tion of administrative remedies, which the State absolutely court. The Supreme Court reversed and said, no, we don't have to reach that issue, the plaintiff here did not exhaust his administrative remedies, and notwithstanding any federal claims and so forth which were briefed and argued, we're not going to waive our exhaustion relief. Counsel, I think, has been most forthright in saying that he cannot speak for the Illinois court, that he cannot assure us that there would be any remedy under 1983 or any other remedy in the Illinois court. And Cromwell has been

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you win -- if you're allowed to main­ tain your action in the federal court and you get an injunc­ tion, you win on your equal protection ground; you wouldn't expect to get interest?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: No, sir. No, sir. The only possibility, Mr. Justice White, under those circumstances would be that under the rationale of Fulton Market v. Cullerton, that we might go back at a later date and sue for whatever other ex­ penses we had. But, note, that under an injunction, under an injunction -- injunction, one, we would not have to pay the unjust moiety.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Secondly, under 1988, we would be entitled to our client's attorneys' fees, so that the client would be made whole with an injunction. She would not have to lay out her money, and her attorneys' fees and costs would be paid.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: And, by the way, there is no such provi­ sion under Illinois law, allowing the so-called rule of attor­ neys' fees in prevailing in a case such as this. There is no Neither, as we pointed out, is there any interest even after j udgment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fox, if the State of Illinois amended its procedure to allow interest at something that may be less in Illinois at the present time. On judgments against the State, it's six percent. The Internal Revenue Service under Section 482 is now talking a range of 11 to 13 percent. I think the court would then have to face a due process question in the amount of interest. If they allowed four percent or five percent in a day when we have a 15-1/2 percent prime, this is another question. I mean, I think it's a -- is it rea­ sonable?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, this isn't a due process question. It's a statutory question --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- of whether or not there exists a plain, speedy, and efficient remedy in the courts of the State.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: But again, and I hate to bring the questior CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the ttached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic ound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court f the United States in the matter of: No. 79-1157 EDWARD J. ROSEWELL, ETC., ET AL. , v. La SALLE NATIONAL BANK, TRUSTEE, ETC. and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court. BY:
